# 📝 Notes

Jun 17, 2026

## 8am AI\!

Invited [David Olsson](mailto:david@atomic47.co) [eric@scroll.ca](mailto:eric@scroll.ca) [giroday@gmail.com](mailto:giroday@gmail.com) [hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com](mailto:hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com) [Fulvio Ciano](mailto:fulvio@atomic47.co) [scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com](mailto:scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com) [Jason Kryski](mailto:jason@mttr.ca) [Jen Boger](mailto:jen@atomic47.co) [Shabraj Shahid](mailto:shabraj@mttr.ca) [spencer@ratiomachina.com](mailto:spencer@ratiomachina.com) [graham@ratiomachina.com](mailto:graham@ratiomachina.com) [Bailey Klinger](mailto:bwklinger@gmail.com) [andre.bertram3@gmail.com](mailto:andre.bertram3@gmail.com) [Spencer MacBeth](mailto:spencer@mttr.ca) [mike@smartdeskcrm.com](mailto:mike@smartdeskcrm.com)

Attachments [8am AI\!](https://calendar.google.com/calendar/event?eid=aW52N2w4MWJvNjJqYm5zYmQxZTMzYjllNmZfMjAyNjA2MTdUMTUwMDAwWiBkYXZpZEBhdG9taWM0Ny5jbw)

Meeting records [Transcript](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1GGQYxm_3Fx6rVgKH2nDyDTBeU5718HQX0fbufnemQAA/edit?usp=drive_web&tab=t.mut3y52gdnqm) 

### Summary

Technical discussions regarding model integration and decentralized storage evolved into strategic implementations of automated project management systems.

**System Integration and Verification**  
Participants examined connecting local models with cloud infrastructure while leveraging GitHub Gists as a secure data substrate for hashing and verification. This approach allows for prototyping distributed chain mechanics and robust data integrity without relying on traditional blockchain complexity.

**Cognitive Database and Analytics**  
The discussion shifted to the Project State system, which organizes knowledge into typed datasets to perform correlative analysis. This framework automates administrative reporting and generates dynamic dashboards that surface critical milestones and risks across disparate organizational tools.

**Administrative Automation and Scalability**  
The group addressed instructional workload management through a plugin that automates curriculum document generation and spreadsheet creation. This system serves as a scalable solution for organizations to manage high-value projects and complex reporting requirements through automated, background workflows.

### Decisions

## Aligned

* **Plugin framework adoption for school projects** The participants aligned on adopting David's plugin and memory framework to automate and structure workflows for Ying's upcoming school projects.

We've **updated the Decisions section** using your feedback.

Let us know what you think: [Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_5p6FWBVWvynleNU?isGoogler=no&isHelpful=yes) or [Not Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_5p6FWBVWvynleNU?isGoogler=no&isHelpful=no)

### Next steps

- [ ] \[Fulvio Ciano\] Test Interconnection: Test the interconnection between the local model and cloud models to confirm the reported fix.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Share project plugin: Send the plugin and relevant access links to the group via email. Include the meeting transcript in the correspondence.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Record instruction video: Create a video demonstrating how to use the plugin. Distribute the recording to the group before next Wednesday.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Explain headless functionality: Discuss the technical details of the headless cloud application with Juan.

### Details

*Did the screenshots in this section make your notes [better](https://goo.gle/44IH7J9) or [worse](https://goo.gle/4b5ZWtJ)?*

* **Ollama and Model Interconnection**: Fulvio Ciano and David Olsson discuss technical challenges regarding the interconnection between local models, specifically Ollama, and cloud models, noting that a recent software update resolved a previous breakage. David Olsson mentions being unable to run the Hermes model on their Intel-based Mac but expresses interest in the pattern of enabling local models with cloud integration ([00:01:02](#00:01:02)).

* **Mind Map Tool and Base64 Encoding**: David Olsson demonstrates a custom mind map tool created via AI prompts, which utilizes Base64 URLs to generate and load maps ([00:02:11](#00:02:11)). David Olsson highlights that this "thin client" pattern allows simple applications to accept structured data, enabling the creation of shareable, immutable maps that are "hardened" by the act of creation ([00:03:12](#00:03:12)).

  ![][image1]

* **Git Gists as Data Substrate**: David Olsson explores using GitHub gists to emulate blockchain-like functionality, creating a system for verifying and hashing data ([00:05:09](#00:05:09)). Fulvio Ciano and David Olsson discuss that while it is not a distributed ledger in the traditional sense, using Gists as a storage substrate allows for securing data, fingerprinting, and manual verification ([00:06:28](#00:06:28)) ([00:09:27](#00:09:27)).

  ![][image2]

* **Automating Data Verification and Signing**: David Olsson details how their setup automates SHA-256 hashing of content to create "proofs" and discusses integrating an "operator in the loop" for manual signing using Mac OS fingerprint authentication ([00:07:43](#00:07:43)). This approach allows for automated background processes while maintaining the option for human verification of critical data ([00:08:38](#00:08:38)).

* **Chain Prototyping and Storage Evolution**: Fulvio Ciano and David Olsson discuss the potential for "chaining" these Gist-based proofs by referencing hashes in subsequent entries. They note that modern, affordable storage makes using Git repositories for this type of research feasible, allowing for prototyping distributed chain mechanics without needing a complex, full-scale blockchain infrastructure ([00:09:27](#00:09:27)) ([00:11:16](#00:11:16)).

* **Transitioning from Discussion to Action**: David Olsson emphasizes the need to move from "talking" about AI to "activating" it, noting that most prototyping currently relies on free databases like Neon or Supabase. The participants reflect on the importance of turning conversational knowledge into actionable outputs within their workflow ([00:13:05](#00:13:05)) ([00:15:12](#00:15:12)).

* **Defining Knowledge vs. Intelligence**: Fulvio Ciano and David Olsson debate the terminology of knowledge, intelligence, and wisdom, acknowledging the lack of precise definitions in the field ([00:14:01](#00:14:01)). They agree that regardless of the terminology, their goal is to transform the unresolved topics discussed in their meetings into actionable tasks or frameworks ([00:15:12](#00:15:12)).

* **Hierarchical Processing of Information**: Ying introduces the data-information-knowledge-wisdom hierarchy, arguing that AI excels at processing data into information and knowledge quickly. Ying contends that human value now lies in exercising wisdom—applying context and experience to that knowledge—while David Olsson notes that static knowledge in files often lacks the "actionability" they seek ([00:16:27](#00:16:27)).

  ![][image3]

* **Categorizing Intelligence for Actionable Plans**: Ying argues that to provide tailored guidance or "software action," knowledge must be categorized into specific intelligence domains (e.g., artistic, interpersonal, mathematical) ([00:20:16](#00:20:16)). David Olsson agrees that taxonomies are helpful but remains focused on identifying a specific, executable function that their meetings can produce beyond just structured information ([00:21:45](#00:21:45)).

* **Collective Memory and Future Value**: David Olsson explores the idea of creating an executable product from their meetings, such as a "Tamagotchi" style interface to nurture conversation flow, or simply finding ways to connect more deeply with the community ([00:23:08](#00:23:08)). The participants acknowledge that they currently "leave everything on the table" and seek to capture the purposeful nature of their weekly discussions ([00:24:21](#00:24:21)).

* **AI-Generated Memoirs and Collective History**: Ying proposes using AI to structure their recorded conversations into a book or memoir, documenting their thoughts on AI's societal impact as a form of "collective memory" ([00:25:21](#00:25:21)). David Olsson notes the need for participant consent, reflecting on previous discussions about how signing such releases fundamentally alters the group's relationship dynamics ([00:28:27](#00:28:27)).

* **Juan’s Perspective on Traveling Memory**: Juan shares a challenge regarding "traveling memory"—the difficulty of carrying knowledge across different life domains like work, family, and friends. Juan proposes a tree-like folder structure for knowledge management, where an orchestrator agent can inject relevant context into different domains to improve decision-making ([00:30:25](#00:30:25)).

* **Structured Domain Management**: Juan contrasts vector database retrieval with their preferred method of using a hierarchical folder structure that mimics actual project domains ([00:33:13](#00:33:13)). Juan provides an example of using this structured approach to determine that Veracode was a cost-effective security solution for meeting SOC 2 compliance, demonstrating how an orchestrator can reason over structured documentation ([00:34:35](#00:34:35)).

* **Introduction to Project State**: David Olsson presents "Project State," a system designed to organize knowledge into typed datasets (decisions, risks, lessons learned) rather than just raw information ([00:37:03](#00:37:03)). David Olsson explains that by structuring memory this way, AI can effectively process the data to derive lessons, track risks, and maintain the integrity of project evolution ([00:39:05](#00:39:05)).

* **Analysis and Dynamic Dashboards**: David Olsson demonstrates the cognitive database capabilities of Project State, which performs correlative and causal analysis on project documentation ([00:41:15](#00:41:15)). The system generates a dynamic "today" dashboard that surfaces actionable tasks, risks, and milestones, automating the management process for the user ([00:42:11](#00:42:11)).

* **Adaptability and UI Evolution**: Ying expresses interest in a personalized dashboard, and David Olsson explains that while the underlying data and skills are structured, the UI is dynamic and evolves based on the content (e.g., research curriculum, student management) ([00:43:04](#00:43:04)). The Project State system adapts to different inputs, effectively managing complex, multi-faceted projects by re-casting information based on the user's current needs ([00:45:04](#00:45:04)).

* **Correlative Analytics and Multi-Platform Harvesting**: David Olsson highlights the system's ability to analyze information for causal relationships and gaps ([00:45:59](#00:45:59)). The platform harvests data from various sources like Slack, Gmail, Jira, and Confluence, allowing for consolidated management and automated weekly retrospectives via the dashboard ([00:47:09](#00:47:09)).

* **Automation of Cross-Organizational Reporting**: David Olsson demonstrates an automated "Monday morning email" skill that summarizes project progress, blockers, and plans across multiple organizations ([00:49:31](#00:49:31)). This allows for effective communication with stakeholders without requiring them to use the same management tools, as the system extracts the necessary Project State information ([00:51:07](#00:51:07)).

* **Headless Implementation and CLI Usage**: David Olsson and Juan discuss using "headless" Claude via the command line interface (CLI) to run Project State, allowing for integration within IDEs like Cursor ([00:52:07](#00:52:07)). David Olsson explains that using the CLI enables them to bypass the UI, interact directly with the intelligence layer, and maintain typed data across multiple collaborators ([00:53:24](#00:53:24)).

* **Adaptability to Research Projects and Local Privacy**: Ying inquires about adapting the system for multiple research projects, and David Olsson confirms that Project State operates on the local file system, ensuring data privacy ([00:54:37](#00:54:37)). The system is modular, allowing users to define milestones, risks, and goals for any project type, with the ability to evolve data structures dynamically using AI agents ([00:56:48](#00:56:48)).

* **Courseware and Curriculum Management**: David Olsson describes a methodology for managing courseware by utilizing a highly structured framework. They explain that by defining policies and rubrics within an LLM (Large Language Model), users can implement changes at the beginning of a process—such as updating a rubric—that automatically propagate through related curriculum documents and handouts ([00:59:34](#00:59:34)).

* **Addressing Instructional Workload**: Ying expresses anxiety regarding their upcoming teaching schedule in July and August, noting they feel overwhelmed. In response, David Olsson introduces a plugin designed to reduce this workload by automating administrative tasks, storing files in a central location, and generating assets like PDF and Excel documents ([01:00:35](#01:00:35)).

* **Plugin Availability and Usage**: David Olsson explains that they have accumulated 41 years of experience with spreadsheets and Gantt charts, leading them to develop tools that automate these tasks rather than manual creation. David Olsson commits to sharing this plugin with the group and producing a video tutorial to demonstrate how to use it ([01:01:35](#01:01:35)) ([01:03:23](#01:03:23)).

* **Pilot Status and System Accessibility**: David Olsson describes the current status of the tool as being in a pilot phase, with participants like Fio and Juan currently testing or building their own versions of the system. David Olsson notes that while the application will be accessible on multiple platforms, the plugin is specifically accessible for Windows users like Ying, and they explain that it functions in the background once implemented ([01:02:34](#01:02:34)).

* **Follow-up and Communication Plan**: David Olsson commits to sending a follow-up email today containing the meeting transcript and a link to the plugin. Furthermore, David Olsson plans to distribute a video to the group before the following Wednesday to facilitate the pilot process for several companies ([01:03:23](#01:03:23)).

* **Dashboarding and Reporting Functionality**: Ying shares that Karen values the project dashboard because it consolidates information and decision-making into one place ([01:03:23](#01:03:23)). David Olsson explains that the system was developed out of necessity for a company called Crush Dynamics, which requires complex, government-funded reporting. The system enables dynamic report generation, allowing users to produce Excel spreadsheets and documentation as a natural output of their work, rather than viewing reports as a separate, static task ([01:04:18](#01:04:18)).

* **Scalability for Small Organizations**: David Olsson discusses how the paradigm of dynamic reporting and structured project management is powerful for small organizations and individuals attempting to execute high-value projects. They explain that this approach allows users to scale their efforts over time, shifting from session-based work to managing long-term, complex projects that the tool helps organize ([01:05:21](#01:05:21)).

*You should review Gemini's notes to make sure they're accurate. [Get tips and learn how Gemini takes notes](https://support.google.com/meet/answer/14754931)*

*How is the quality of **these specific notes?** [Take a short survey](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_9vK3UZEaIQKKE7A?confid=WquJk93O1skiT2zOXFP2DxITOAIIigIgABgBCA&detailid=standard&screenshot=true) to let us know your feedback, including how helpful the notes were for your needs.*

Ying: Hi everyone.

### 00:16:27 {#00:16:27}

Ying: Um,

David Olsson: Good morning.

Ying: morning. Okay. It's so funny.

Juan: Morning.

Ying: I I clicked the link today and it said it's a dangerous website. Are you sure you want to proceed? I'm like, what? Just the link you gave me. It gave me It said the dangerous website. Anyway, um I just overheard the the argument now. I mean the the statement about the knowledge. So I don't know whether you guys are aware of this hierarchy. So data, information, knowledge and wisdom. So data basically is the daytoday things and happen in the real world. And when you organize the data in a certain structure become information which is usable and you further um filtering the information into um uh things that can help you make uh decisions that become knowledge. And when you further uh fine-tune knowledge into um theory like can guide your actions that becomes wisdom. So I think that's the that's why the concept become complicated is there's multiple layers from data to information to knowledge to wisdom. I think in AI age knowledge going to become commodity.

### 00:17:51

Ying: It's really the wisdom like humans can extract knowledge and make that into wisdom is the only reason we have value to contribute. That's my taking on because AI agent can store data and uh create information create knowledge in seconds. It's the knowledge transfer into wisdom because need context, need experience, need human uh input. Um that's probably the only place we can still contribute to certain extent. Um that's my understanding about it.

David Olsson: Yeah, there's there's there's theories on what the stack looks like and where wisdom and intelligence and all those things line up, but some of them are um some of them just become layers that others consume. And and in many cases, anything that's in a file is is is not intelligent. it's just knowledge. Um, and so if we say that we have all these transcripts, we can say we have all this knowledge collected. We can call it information and then we can put it in a mind map and it's knowledge. And once it's in that state though, it's still static. It's not doing anything.

### 00:34:35 {#00:34:35}

David Olsson: whatever the memory file system that you create, it's how you create it that makes those memories useful for for the AI. And that's what project state does. And and what Juan is uh describing is exactly how the skills work on top of it. They continue to evolve, but they enable what's called a typed data set. So that when you're storing a memory, it's stored in a certain type of way. So that all of them get processed really easily together. And then they're structured in the way that they're used. So when he's talking documentation, it's used in a certain way. If he's talking product information, product is actually different than than the actual software, right? ### 00:37:54

David Olsson: And project state was designed to understand from running any kind of project like from beginning to end. Oh, an assignment is a project um that you require not the raw information. Ying: Coffee.

David Olsson: very similar. So you load up a project and there's there's as many that you can. So um in this case it's actually the the state for project state which is um project itself. But if I go into into more here I'll show you the the system no the uh shift substrate.

### 00:39:05 {#00:39:05}

David Olsson: Um these are all the files and and they're and they're they're written in okay let's go to some that are easier there. These are all the files. This is the memory and the memory is not just like data but it's functional data and how it's structured and so the project state itself figures out what your content is and in the case of my stuff is like once it's all software related project there's these things we can add repos to it and all these other things but what it's doing is it's it's actually figuring out lessons learned by the team by studying what happened in Slack and whatnot it's not bringing the Slack message in it's studying the Slack message saying hey you guys decided on that that's a memory that's a decision ision, it goes in our decision folder and our decisions are all tracked and they actually are related to other things like risks. And so you take your decisions and your risks and you combine them together and you analyze those. And so if we were to do that with our conversations like we could say themes and then we can actually say personal people have their own interests and then you can say there's collisions to things too and you can say that lessons learned over time and so if you collect things in terms of the information in the structured way that one is mentioning and that I've here then you can actually say okay you've got all these knowledge objects of certain kinds and

### 00:40:18

David Olsson: then you capture them over time so that you can start doing monthly yearly and then you gain that understanding of things um over time and that's a layer on top of that and then you can do the same thing with themes and so you start layering this information um and they become more specific as you start to understand them but you can see like look at these onboarding this wasn't there before this is the ability to onboard anybody into the project and there's a lot of notes associated with that but phases of a project are managed and these things are generated from project state but they're also generated from the the needs that you bring to it. And that's the really neat thing with the LLM. You talked about this earlier. Um, you it's really good at some of these things. And and one of the things it's really good at is taking information in one structure and casting it into another structure. And I would call it information at this point just to to mute the conversation earlier.

### 00:41:15 {#00:41:15}

David Olsson: um and say that at this point it's in a really structured format, really well designed for software and really well designed for the LLM because it can what Juan was saying it can reason over it And so I call it a cognitive database. None of these none of these files mean much on their own. Some of them are reports. They do mean things like this MD here, lessons learned. That's a report. You can see that goes to humans if we wanted to. None of these things mean anything on their own, but together given there's a map schema that ties them together. Like here's the over here's the milestones and here's the risks. They're tied together. Um you you you you send them to the LLM together and you say analyze this for where we're at. Does this affect our timeline? And back in the day, you'd do that in Microsoft Project with end and beginning dates and do all kinds of math. What it's doing is it's actually analyzing things because it said in your conversation, you agreed to this.

David Olsson: um analyze things that aren't just in information. it it actually takes your information and it can and does correlative and causal analysis on it. And um and that's what you need.

### 00:47:09 {#00:47:09}

David Olsson: It analyzes things according to the milestones the risks and everything else it has. And then it gives you actions that you can do. You can you can do you can go harvest from your Gmail your inbox. Um you could update your progress which you can do manually or it will do it behind the scenes when it actually does the harvesting. And you can review all of the the risks associated with the project because I'm the only developer. There's like some pretty good risks, but I can go review those and then if I want to take action, I can escalate or I can capture a lesson learned, meaning that this is not a good idea. So the the the app does have fully generated, fully actionable chat experience, but it's also UI. David Olsson: Um, this thing again it has all these skills like there's 35 of them. They all coordinate run together. You don't need to run and know most of that, but I'm building this, so I do. You can add a folder or a website um or a repo and it will observe these things and if anything changes it will study them and then make notes of it in the in the software in the in the substrate. Um but it it has common views on things but it also you know so that you have got a calendar and I can actually add like a retrospective into it and um and then so that'll trigger weekly or daily and so it has full automation of of all the skills involved too um and that becomes handy when say you need to communicate to people um uh on a cadence right so I have projects the projects we meet weekly and and we have retrospectives on Friday and Monday mornings I queue up an email that is built on top of project state and it and it could be course curriculum.
